phase.title: Startup Apps
phase.summary: Startup folder and Run-key targets that fire on next interactive admin logon.

nodes.title: Writable global startup folder
nodes.summary: Drop a payload in `C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\StartUp` — runs at any user's logon.
nodes.description_md:
The All-Users Startup folder (`C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\StartUp`) causes any executable or shortcut placed there to run automatically when **any** user logs in interactively. If a non-admin user can write to this directory, they can plant a payload that executes in the context of the next user (including admins) who logs on.

**Check**: `icacls "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup"` — look for `(W)`, `(M)`, or `(F)` for `Everyone`, `BUILTIN\Users`, or `NT AUTHORITY\Authenticated Users`.

**Payload types**:
- Executable (`.exe`, `.bat`, `.ps1`) — most reliable
- Shortcut (`.lnk`) — stealthier, can use legitimate icon
- VBScript (`.vbs`) — bypasses some .exe restrictions

**Escalation**: If an administrator is expected to log in after you (e.g., you have a low-priv foothold on an exam box), planting here gives you their token on login.
nodes.detection_hints: Writes to `C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\...\Startup` by non-admin accounts; new files with executable extensions in the All-Users startup folder; EID 4688 for processes launched from startup folder paths.

nodes.title: Writable Run / RunOnce key target
nodes.summary: Run keys point at binaries; if that binary or its directory is writable, swap it.
nodes.description_md:
Run keys in the registry (`HKLM\...\Run` and `HKCU\...\Run`) execute named binaries at user logon. Rather than modifying the key itself (which usually requires admin for HKLM), the target is the **binary referenced by an existing run key entry**. If that binary is in a writable directory, overwriting it is sufficient.

**Steps**:
1. Enumerate run key entries: `reg query HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`
2. Note each entry's binary path
3. Check writability: `icacls "<binary_path>"` or `accesschk -quvw <binary_path>`
4. Overwrite with payload (back up the original)
5. Wait for admin logon or trigger a logoff/logon cycle

**Scope**: HKLM run keys run for all users. HKCU keys run for the specific user whose hive they're in — not directly useful for escalation unless you can write to another user's HKCU.
nodes.detection_hints: File writes to run-key-referenced binary paths by non-admin accounts; FIM alerts on autostart binary locations; process creation from autorun paths with unexpected content.

nodes.title: AutoStart services with weak ACLs
nodes.summary: Cross-link to the service-misconfig phase: auto-start services run at boot as their principal.
nodes.description_md:
Auto-start services (Start type = 2 in registry) execute at system boot in the security context of their configured service account (often SYSTEM). This is a cross-reference node to the service misconfigurations phase — the key insight is that **auto-start** services are particularly valuable targets because:

1. They launch without any user interaction (no waiting for logon)
2. They often run as SYSTEM or a privileged service account
3. A writable binary/ACL is exploitable at the next reboot

**Prioritization tip**: When both a demand-start and an auto-start service have the same misconfiguration, target the auto-start service first — it will execute on the next system restart or maintenance window without requiring a user to log in.

**Enumeration**: `Get-CimInstance Win32_Service | Where-Object { $_.StartMode -eq 'Auto' -and $_.PathName -notlike 'C:\Windows\*' }` filters for auto-start third-party services.
nodes.detection_hints: Correlate auto-start service binary modifications (see svc-weak-binary-acl) with unexpected process creation at system startup; EID 7036 (service state change) for unexpected service transitions.

nodes.title: All-Users Startup Folder Write Access
nodes.summary: Place a malicious executable or shortcut in the All-Users Startup folder to execute as every user who logs in, including administrators.
nodes.description_md:
The All-Users Startup folder (`C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup`) executes its contents for every user on login. If this directory grants write access to non-admin users, an attacker can plant a binary or LNK file that runs in the context of each logging-in user.

Contrast with per-user startup (`%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup`) which only escalates if a higher-privileged user logs in.

Check: `icacls "C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup"`
nodes.detection_hints: File integrity monitoring on All-Users Startup folder. Alert on writes by non-admin accounts. Monitor Event ID 4688 for process creation from startup folder paths.

snippets.title: Check ACL and plant payload
snippets.description: Check the All-Users Startup folder ACL, then drop payload for execution on next login.

snippets.title: PowerShell ACL check
snippets.description: Use PowerShell to check if non-admin groups can write to the All-Users Startup folder.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder

nodes.title: Active Setup StubPath Abuse
nodes.summary: Register a malicious StubPath under HKCU Active Setup key to execute arbitrary commands during user login, leveraging the Active Setup mechanism that runs once-per-user.
nodes.description_md:
Active Setup (`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components\`) runs StubPath commands once per user per component version on login. The mechanism compares version strings between HKLM and HKCU entries and re-runs the StubPath if the HKLM version is newer.

A low-privileged user can write to `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components\` to override version strings, or an admin can plant HKLM entries. The StubPath runs in the user's context, but if combined with an HKLM write (e.g., via `svc-registry-acl`), can target elevated users.

This technique is also used for persistence that survives user logoff.
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor HKLM Active Setup key for new or modified entries. Alert on StubPath values pointing to user-writable directories. Review Event ID 4657 (registry value modified) for Active Setup paths.

snippets.title: Enumerate existing Active Setup entries
snippets.description: List all existing Active Setup StubPath values to identify the pattern.

snippets.title: Plant malicious StubPath in HKLM
snippets.code:
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components\{DEADBEEF-1337-1337-1337-DEADBEEF1337}" /v StubPath /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Temp\payload.exe" /f
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components\{DEADBEEF-1337-1337-1337-DEADBEEF1337}" /v Version /t REG_SZ /d "1,0,0,0" /f
snippets.description: Register a new Active Setup component with a malicious StubPath executed on next user login.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Active Setup

nodes.title: UserInitMprLogonScript Registry Logon Script
nodes.summary: Set the UserInitMprLogonScript registry value under HKCU to execute a script at user logon, or abuse writable HKLM equivalents for system-wide logon script execution.
nodes.description_md:
Windows executes scripts defined in `HKCU\Environment\UserInitMprLogonScript` at user logon via the Winlogon process. Any user can set this for their own session, making it a trivial persistence mechanism.

For privilege escalation: if the attacker can write to another user's `HKCU` hive (e.g., via a backup/restore abuse or profile loading), they can set the script for that higher-privileged user.

Alternatively, `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Userinit` (see `start-winlogon-userinit`) covers SYSTEM-level variants.
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor `HKCU\Environment\UserInitMprLogonScript` for unexpected values. Alert on script execution from this registry trigger by non-standard users. Correlate with Event ID 4624 (logon) and subsequent process creation.

snippets.title: Set logon script for current user
snippets.description: Register a logon script for the current user that executes on every login.

snippets.title: Enumerate logon script values
snippets.description: Enumerate UserInitMprLogonScript across all loaded user hives.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Boot or Logon Initialization Scripts: Logon Script (Windows)

nodes.title: GPO Logon Script Abuse
nodes.summary: Modify or replace a Group Policy logon script referenced in SYSVOL or local policy when the script file or its directory has weak ACLs.
nodes.description_md:
Group Policy can assign logon scripts to users/computers via `User Configuration > Windows Settings > Scripts (Logon/Logoff)`. These scripts are stored in `\\<domain>\SYSVOL\<domain>\Policies\{GPO-GUID}\User\Scripts\Logon\` or locally in `C:\Windows\System32\GroupPolicy\User\Scripts\Logon\`.

If the script file or its containing directory is writable (misconfigured SYSVOL ACLs, or local policy on a workstation), the attacker can replace the script content to execute code when any user in scope logs on.

Local Group Policy script location: `C:\Windows\System32\GroupPolicy\User\Scripts\Logon\`
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor SYSVOL script directories for unauthorized writes. Alert on Group Policy script modifications (gp processing events). Audit GPO script file ACLs during security assessments.

snippets.title: Enumerate GPO logon scripts
snippets.description: List all logon scripts configured through local and domain Group Policy.

snippets.title: Check ACL on GPO script directory
snippets.description: Verify write permissions on the logon script directories.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Boot or Logon Initialization Scripts: Logon Script (Windows)

nodes.title: WMI Permanent Event Subscription Persistence to Privilege
nodes.summary: Create a WMI permanent event subscription (EventFilter + EventConsumer + FilterToConsumerBinding) that executes a payload when a trigger condition is met, surviving reboots.
nodes.description_md:
WMI permanent subscriptions persist in the WMI repository (`%SystemRoot%\System32\wbem\Repository`) and survive reboots. They consist of three objects:
1. `__EventFilter`: WQL query defining the trigger (e.g., user logon, process creation)
2. `CommandLineEventConsumer` or `ActiveScriptEventConsumer`: action to execute
3. `__FilterToConsumerBinding`: links filter to consumer

CommandLineEventConsumer runs as SYSTEM. This provides both persistence and privilege escalation from any user able to create WMI subscriptions (default: administrators only, but misconfigured namespace ACLs may allow others).
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor WMI subscription creation (Event ID 5857, 5860, 5861 in Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational). Enumerate `__EventFilter`, `__EventConsumer`, `__FilterToConsumerBinding` in `root\subscription` namespace regularly.

snippets.title: Create WMI permanent event subscription
snippets.code:
$filterName = 'WindowsUpdateCheck'
$consumerName = 'WindowsUpdateCheck'
$payload = 'C:\Temp\payload.exe'

# Create event filter (trigger on user logon)
$filter = Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\subscription -Class __EventFilter -Arguments @{
    Name = $filterName
    EventNamespace = 'root\cimv2'
    QueryLanguage = 'WQL'
    Query = "SELECT * FROM __InstanceCreationEvent WITHIN 5 WHERE TargetInstance ISA 'Win32_LogonSession'"
}

# Create command-line consumer
$consumer = Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\subscription -Class CommandLineEventConsumer -Arguments @{
    Name = $consumerName
    ExecutablePath = $payload
    CommandLineTemplate = $payload
}

# Bind filter to consumer
Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\subscription -Class __FilterToConsumerBinding -Arguments @{
    Filter = $filter
    Consumer = $consumer
}
snippets.description: Create a WMI permanent subscription that executes a payload on user logon.

snippets.title: Enumerate existing WMI subscriptions
snippets.code:
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class __EventFilter | Select Name, Query
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class CommandLineEventConsumer | Select Name, ExecutablePath
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class ActiveScriptEventConsumer | Select Name, ScriptText
snippets.description: List all existing WMI permanent event subscriptions for detection/audit.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Event Triggered Execution: Windows Management Instrumentation Event Subscription

nodes.title: RunOnce / RunOnceEx Registry Persistence
nodes.summary: Write payloads to RunOnce or RunOnceEx registry keys that execute on next user logon and are deleted after running—useful for one-shot payload delivery or stealthy bootstrapping.
nodes.description_md:
RunOnce keys execute their values once on the next logon and auto-delete:
- `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` (current user)
- `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` (all users, requires admin write)

RunOnceEx (`RunOnceEx`) supports DLL-based execution and additional flags. Prefixing a value with `!` prevents deletion on failure; `*` forces execution even in safe mode.

Distinct from `start-run-keys` which is persistent; RunOnce is single-shot but evades baseline comparisons that only check Run keys.
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor RunOnce key writes. Alert on value prefixes `!` or `*`. Correlate RunOnce execution (logon) with subsequent suspicious process chains.

snippets.title: Write to HKCU RunOnce
snippets.description: Register a single-execution payload in the current user's RunOnce key.

snippets.title: Write to HKLM RunOnceEx (all users, requires elevated write)
snippets.code:
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnceEx\0001" /v Payload /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Temp\payload.dll" /f
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnceEx\0001" /ve /t REG_SZ /d "Windows Update Component" /f
snippets.description: Use RunOnceEx to execute a DLL on next logon for all users.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder

nodes.title: Shortcut (.lnk) Hijack in Startup Folder
nodes.summary: Replace or modify a .lnk shortcut in a Startup folder to redirect execution to an attacker-controlled binary while maintaining the appearance of a legitimate autostart entry.
nodes.description_md:
Startup folders commonly contain .lnk shortcut files that point to legitimate programs. If the Startup folder or the .lnk file itself is writable, an attacker can:
1. Replace the .lnk file with one pointing to a malicious binary
2. Modify the `TargetPath` within an existing .lnk
3. Create a new .lnk that executes a payload then launches the legitimate target (stager pattern)

LNK files can also use relative paths and environment variables, enabling subtle path manipulation. Use `$shell = New-Object -ComObject WScript.Shell` to create/modify LNK files in PowerShell.
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor .lnk file writes in Startup folders. Alert on LNK target path changes. Binary analysis of .lnk files at startup: compare target hash against known-good baseline.

snippets.title: Create malicious .lnk in startup folder
snippets.description: Create a .lnk in the All-Users Startup folder pointing to the attacker payload, using a legitimate-looking icon.

snippets.title: Enumerate and audit existing startup shortcuts
snippets.description: List all .lnk files in Startup folders and their targets for audit.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
